one_line_takeaway: UnSLU-BENCH is the first speaker-forgetting machine-unlearning benchmark for spoken language understanding (4 languages, 4 datasets, 2 models, 8 methods), and its proposed GUM metric - which jointly grounds efficacy (MIA), utility (macro F1), and efficiency (retrain-time ratio) against a gold model - shows Negative Gradients to be the best overall trade-off, exposing that efficiency-blind metrics like NoMUS overrate slow or even privacy-degrading methods.
key_finding: Negative Gradients (NG) is the most well-rounded method: it consistently attains the highest GUM, beating the second-best by +35% on FSC and +26% on SLURP* (wav2vec 2.0) and by +39% on ITALIC and SpeechMASSIVE de-DE and +48% on SpeechMASSIVE fr-FR (XLS-R 53). Its advantage comes almost entirely from efficiency (speedups reaching 1748x over retraining on SLURP* wav2vec 2.0) combined with MIA close to the gold model. The paper's central methodological point is that metrics ignoring any one of efficacy/efficiency/utility admit trivial 'unlearners' (the original model, the retrained model, or a random predictor), and that existing metrics like NoMUS are misleading: UNSIR scores NoMUS=.700 yet GUM=0 because its MIA is worse than the original model, and NG vs SCRUB have near-identical NoMUS despite NG being 1748x faster vs SCRUB's 65x (GUM .563 vs .429). A secondary finding: longer original-model training hurts forgettability - at 60 training epochs the unlearned model reaches near-gold utility (F1_T .696 vs gold .707) but its MIA (.611) stays near the original (.628), indicating persistent memorization; the best trade-off is around 11 epochs (F1_T .499, MIA .480).
core_contribution: The paper introduces UnSLU-BENCH, described by the authors as the first benchmark for machine unlearning (MU) in spoken language understanding (SLU). Its own contributions are four-fold: (1) a benchmark spanning four intent-classification datasets in four languages, with two transformer models per dataset; (2) an empirical evaluation of eight unlearning techniques for the specific task of removing an individual speaker's data (framed as a GDPR/CCPA 'right to be forgotten' request); (3) GUM (Global Unlearning Metric), a novel single metric that combines unlearning efficacy, efficiency, and utility simultaneously and grounds all measurements against a 'gold' model retrained from scratch; and (4) an analysis of how unlearning performance varies with dataset complexity, language, model size/architecture, learning rate, and original-model training duration. The eight unlearning methods themselves (FT, NG, NG+, CF-k, UNSIR, BT/BT-L, SCRUB) are all drawn from prior work; the paper's own novelty is the SLU benchmark, the speaker-forgetting setup, and the GUM metric, not the unlearning algorithms.
threat_model: Privacy / regulatory-compliance setting rather than an adversarial-attack setting. A speech model (e.g., a voice assistant doing intent classification) is trained on data from a set of speakers S. A subset of speakers Sf exercises their 'right to be forgotten' and requests deletion of their data (the forget set Df). Full retraining on the retain set Dr (the 'gold' model) is the ideal but is computationally infeasible per request, so an unlearning algorithm must produce a model that approximates the gold model. The adversarial component is a Membership Inference Attack (MIA): the benchmark uses MIA success as the efficacy proxy for whether a forgotten speaker's data can still be inferred from the model, i.e. whether the deletion was genuine. The concern is persistent memorization of personally identifiable speech data despite a nominal deletion.
limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that GUM (and the whole evaluation) requires access to the gold model retrained from scratch, which is generally available only at validation time and not in deployment; they note no gold-free solution exists for the field. Efficacy is measured solely via MIA, and the paper itself cites work ([33]) warning that inexact unlearning needs more careful evaluation to avoid a 'false sense of privacy', so MIA-based efficacy is an imperfect proxy. Scope is limited to intent-classification SLU on transformer encoders (wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, XLS-R 128/53); SpeechMASSIVE covers 12 languages but only German and French are used. The forget set is speaker-level and modest (2.5-5% of data); scaling to many simultaneous deletion requests or other forgetting granularities is not tested. Results are strongly dataset- and model-dependent (e.g., Bad Teaching works on FSC/SLURP but poorly on multilingual XLS-R; NG+ catastrophically forgets on XLS-R 128), so the 'NG is best' conclusion is a benchmark-wide tendency, not universal.

datasets_used: SLURP (English, 60 intents) - re-split by the authors into speaker-independent SLURP*
SpeechMASSIVE (German de-DE and French fr-FR), 60 intents
quantitative_results: NG highest GUM: +35% over second-best on FSC and +26% on SLURP* (wav2vec 2.0); +39% on ITALIC and SpeechMASSIVE de-DE and +48% on SpeechMASSIVE fr-FR (XLS-R 53)
NG speedup over retraining reaches 1748x (SLURP*, wav2vec 2.0; Tables 2 and 4); 206.9x on FSC wav2vec 2.0

GUM vs NoMUS (SLURP*, wav2vec 2.0, Table 4): NG GUM=.563 vs SCRUB GUM=.429 despite similar NoMUS (.744 vs .741); UNSIR NoMUS=.700 but GUM=.000 (MIA .637 worse than original .628); Original NoMUS=.717 and Gold NoMUS=.848 both give GUM=.000
NG+ catastrophic forgetting on XLS-R 128: forget-set F1_F=.001 on ITALIC and .008 on SpeechMASSIVE fr-FR
FT near-gold utility on XLS-R 128 ITALIC: F1_T=.638 vs gold .643; FT speedups range 7.96x-83.78x

Training-duration study (SLURP*, wav2vec 2.0, NG+, Table 5): 60 epochs -> F1_T .696 (gold .707) but MIA .611 near original .628, GUM .421; 11 epochs -> F1_T .499, MIA .480 (gold .492), GUM .686 (peak)
CF-k incomplete unlearning: MIA .612-.624 vs gold .493-.520 on SpeechMASSIVE de-DE/fr-FR
Forget set Df is 2.5-5% of training data (speakers with >=100 samples each)
baselines_compared: Catastrophic forgetting of last k layers (CF-k)
Bad Teaching (BT) and its light variant (BT-L)
Reference points: Original model and Gold (retrain-from-scratch) model
Metric baseline: NoMUS (compared against the proposed GUM)
